claim: Senior engineers struggle with agents because they carry implicit context about APIs and systems that agents don't have; a deleteItem endpoint is obvious to you but opaque to the model
actionable: Write agent-ready APIs with self-documenting schemas and detailed docstrings; don't assume the agent has years of implicit context
source_title: Why (Senior) Engineers Struggle to Build AI Agents

claim: Text is the new state: replace Boolean flags and structured data with semantic text that LLMs can understand and augment (e.g., approve plan with additional constraints)
actionable: Use natural language for agent state representation instead of enums/flags; allow users to provide semantic modifications to text state
source_title: Why (Senior) Engineers Struggle to Build AI Agents

claim: Errors are inputs, not restart triggers: agents running 15 minutes shouldn't lose context on failure; treat errors as normal model inputs and ask for workarounds
actionable: Design agents to handle errors gracefully by feeding error messages back to the model; avoid restarting long-running processes
source_title: Why (Senior) Engineers Struggle to Build AI Agents

claim: Move from unit tests to evals: agents are non-deterministic; test how often something works (e.g., success rate), not whether fixed input always produces fixed output
actionable: Replace unit tests with eval frameworks that measure success rate across runs; use LLM-as-judge or human feedback for qualitative outcomes
source_title: Why (Senior) Engineers Struggle to Build AI Agents

claim: Build to delete: agent systems are disposable and will be rebuilt with better models anyway; don't over-engineer architecture
actionable: Optimize for iteration speed over architectural perfection; assume you'll rewrite the same agent multiple times as models improve
source_title: Why (Senior) Engineers Struggle to Build AI Agents
